value.notes: Crowned at Westminster Abbey.

people_involved.role: Crowned King of England

summary.value: Edward III was crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey on 25 January 1327, following the forced abdication of his father Edward II. He was fourteen years old at the time of his coronation. The early years of his reign were dominated by the regency of his mother Isabella of France and her favourite Roger Mortimer, whom Edward displaced in a coup in October 1330 and thereafter governed in his own right.

title: Marriage of Edward III and Philippa

value.notes: Married at York Minster.

people_involved.role: Groom
Bride

summary.value: Edward III married Philippa of Hainault at York Minster on 24 January 1328. The match had been arranged in 1326 as part of Edward's mother Isabella's diplomatic efforts with the Low Countries. Philippa was the daughter of William I, Count of Hainault and Holland. The marriage proved durable and produced twelve children, five of whose descendants became the principal claimants in the dynastic disputes of the fifteenth century.

value.notes: Edward III's formal claim to the French throne is conventionally dated to 1337.

people_involved.role: Claimant to the French throne; initiator of the war

summary.value: The conflict known as the Hundred Years War began in 1337 when Philip VI of France confiscated the duchy of Gascony from Edward III. Edward responded by renewing his claim to the French throne through his mother Isabella, daughter of Philip IV of France. The war, fought intermittently until 1453, shaped English military culture, fiscal institutions, and continental policy for over a century.

people_involved.role: English commander

summary.value: The Battle of Crécy, fought on 26 August 1346 in northern France, was a decisive English victory over the French forces of Philip VI. The battle demonstrated the tactical effectiveness of the English longbow against armoured cavalry. Edward III commanded the English army; his son Edward of Woodstock (the Black Prince) commanded the vanguard. The victory opened the way for the siege and capture of Calais (1347).

value.notes: The plague reached the south coast in summer 1348 and spread rapidly northward.

summary.value: The Black Death reached England in the summer of 1348, entering through the southern ports, and spread rapidly through the country over the following year. Estimates of mortality vary widely but modern scholarship broadly accepts that between a third and a half of the English population died in the initial outbreak of 1348–49. The pandemic had profound economic, social, and religious consequences, accelerating the decline of villeinage and transforming the labour market.

value.notes: Created by letters patent.

people_involved.role: Created Duke of Clarence by Edward III

summary.value: On 13 November 1362 Edward III created his son Lionel of Antwerp Duke of Clarence by letters patent — the first use of that title in English history. The title was derived from the honour of Clare in Suffolk, which had come to Lionel through his wife Elizabeth de Burgh. Lionel was simultaneously serving as Lieutenant of Ireland, a role he held from 1361 to 1366.

people_involved.role: Lieutenant of Ireland; presided over the Irish Parliament that enacted the statute

summary.value: The Statute of Kilkenny (1366) was a body of legislation enacted during Lionel of Antwerp's lieutenancy of Ireland. It forbade the Anglo-Irish settler community from adopting Gaelic Irish customs, language, law, or from intermarrying with the Gaelic population. Historians regard the statute as evidence of the anxiety felt by the English administration about the integration of settlers into Gaelic society rather than as an effective piece of governance: its practical enforcement was limited, and the anglicised areas contracted further in the following century.

value.notes: Died at Alba, Piedmont.

people_involved.role: Deceased

summary.value: Lionel of Antwerp, Duke of Clarence and third surviving son of Edward III, died on 17 October 1368 at Alba in Piedmont, Italy, several months after his second marriage to Violante Visconti. The cause of death is uncertain. His death without male heirs meant that the Plantagenet descent through Lionel passed to his daughter Philippa, making her marriage to Edmund Mortimer, 3rd Earl of March, the critical genealogical link for the future Yorkist claim.

value.notes: Died at Windsor Castle.

people_involved.role: Deceased

summary.value: Philippa of Hainault, Queen Consort of England, died at Windsor Castle on 15 August 1369. She had been queen for over forty years and bore Edward III twelve children. Her death preceded Edward's by eight years; in his final years the king's court was dominated by Alice Perrers, his mistress, a situation which attracted widespread criticism from contemporaries.

value.notes: Died at Sheen Palace, Surrey.

people_involved.role: Deceased

summary.value: Edward III died at Sheen Palace, Surrey, on 21 June 1377, in the fiftieth year of his reign. He was succeeded by his grandson Richard II, son of the Black Prince who had predeceased him. Edward's death crystallised the dynastic question that would define the following century: his five sons had left multiple lines of descent whose competing claims to seniority became the basis for the Lancastrian and Yorkist positions in the Wars of the Roses.

people_involved.role: Norman commander; won the English crown

summary.value: The Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066 was one of the most consequential military engagements in English history. William, Duke of Normandy, defeated and killed King Harold II, the last Anglo-Saxon king of England, and secured the English throne. The battle lasted approximately nine hours; Harold was killed, traditionally said to be by an arrow to the eye. William's victory inaugurated Norman rule and permanently altered the English language, culture, aristocracy, and Church.

people_involved.role: Commissioned the survey

summary.value: The Domesday Book was a comprehensive survey of England commissioned by William I in 1085 and compiled in 1086, recording the landholding, ownership, and taxable value of virtually every settlement in England south of the Tees. It was the most ambitious administrative undertaking in medieval Europe to that date. The survey served as a definitive record of landholding following the Norman Conquest and was used for centuries as a legal reference. Its name derives from the thoroughness of its coverage, likened by contemporaries to the Last Judgement (Doomsday).

value.notes: Stephen crowned at Westminster, 22 December 1135, within three weeks of Henry I's death.

people_involved.role: Seized the throne; crowned King of England
Designated heir of Henry I; rival claimant

summary.value: On the death of Henry I on 1 December 1135, his nephew Stephen of Blois raced to England, secured the treasury at Winchester, and was crowned within three weeks — bypassing Henry's designated heir, Empress Matilda. The resulting civil war, known as The Anarchy, plunged England into nearly twenty years of baronial conflict. Neither side achieved decisive victory. The crisis was finally resolved by the Treaty of Wallingford in 1153, which recognised Stephen as king for life and appointed Matilda's son Henry as his heir — inaugurating the Plantagenet dynasty.

value.notes: Killed in Canterbury Cathedral, 29 December 1170.

people_involved.role: King of England; whose words were interpreted as ordering the murder

summary.value: Thomas Becket, Archbishop of Canterbury, was murdered in his own cathedral on 29 December 1170 by four knights who took literally Henry II's furious outburst 'Will no one rid me of this turbulent priest?' The killing provoked outrage across Christendom. Pope Alexander III excommunicated the knights and placed England under interdict. Henry II performed public penance at Becket's tomb in 1174 and walked barefoot through the streets of Canterbury to be scourged by the monks. Becket was canonised in 1173. The episode severely damaged Henry's reputation and energised his enemies, contributing to the great revolt of 1173–74 in which his own sons rebelled against him.

value.notes: Parliament convened January 1265; the first to include elected knights and burgesses alongside barons and clergy.

people_involved.role: King of England; nominally presiding

summary.value: In January 1265, Simon de Montfort, Earl of Leicester, convened a parliament in the name of the captive Henry III that for the first time summoned not only magnates and prelates but also two elected knights from each shire and two elected burgesses from each major town. Though Montfort was killed at the Battle of Evesham in August 1265 and his movement crushed, his parliament established a precedent that representation of the commons was part of the political nation. This model was consolidated by Edward I and is conventionally regarded as the origin of the English Parliament.

value.notes: Statute of Rhuddlan (Statute of Wales) issued March 1284 after Edward I's second Welsh war.

people_involved.role: King of England; conqueror of Wales

summary.value: Following the death of Llywelyn ap Gruffudd in battle in 1282 and the suppression of a final Welsh revolt in 1283, Edward I issued the Statute of Wales (Statute of Rhuddlan) in March 1284, incorporating Wales into the English legal system and dividing it into English-style shires. Edward's construction of a ring of massive castles — Harlech, Beaumaris, Caernarfon, and Conwy — permanently secured English dominion. In 1301 Edward presented his infant son Edward (born at Caernarfon) to the Welsh as their prince, establishing the tradition of the heir apparent bearing the title Prince of Wales.

people_involved.role: Sealed Magna Carta under baronial pressure

summary.value: Magna Carta ('the Great Charter') was sealed by King John at Runnymede on 15 June 1215 under pressure from a coalition of rebellious barons. The charter established the principle that the king was subject to the rule of law and could not imprison subjects or seize property without lawful process; its core provisions, particularly clauses 39–40, became the foundation of the common law tradition of due process. Pope Innocent III annulled it within weeks, triggering the First Barons' War, but subsequent reissues (1216, 1217, 1225) gave it permanent legal standing.

people_involved.role: English commander; suffered decisive defeat

summary.value: The Battle of Bannockburn (23–24 June 1314) was the decisive engagement of the First War of Scottish Independence. A Scottish army under Robert Bruce routed the English forces of Edward II, who was attempting to relieve Stirling Castle. The battle secured Scottish independence in practice and led ultimately to the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328), by which England recognised Scotland as an independent kingdom.

people_involved.role: English commander; decisive victory over France

summary.value: The Battle of Agincourt on 25 October 1415 (St Crispin's Day) was the most celebrated English military victory of the Hundred Years War. Henry V's army, significantly outnumbered and weakened by disease, annihilated a French force of superior size through the disciplined use of longbowmen. The victory enabled Henry's conquest of Normandy and his recognition as heir to the French throne by the Treaty of Troyes (1420). Immortalised in Shakespeare's Henry V, the battle became a defining element of English national mythology.

The Wars of the Roses began with the First Battle of St Albans on 22 May 1455, when Yorkist forces under Richard, Duke of York, routed a Lancastrian army and captured King Henry VI. The conflict — a series of intermittent civil wars between the Lancastrian and Yorkist factions, both descended from sons of Edward III — lasted until the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485. The name 'Wars of the Roses' is a Victorian coinage derived from the heraldic badges of the two factions: the red rose of Lancaster and the white rose of York.

people_involved.role: Yorkist king; killed in battle
Victorious claimant; became King of England

summary.value: The Battle of Bosworth Field on 22 August 1485 ended the Wars of the Roses and the Plantagenet era. Richard III was killed in the fighting — the last English king to die in battle — and Henry Tudor's victory established the Tudor dynasty. Richard's crown was reportedly found on the battlefield and placed on Henry's head by Lord Stanley. Henry's subsequent marriage to Elizabeth of York united the Lancastrian and Yorkist claims and symbolically ended the dynastic conflict.

people_involved.role: Declared Supreme Head of the Church of England

summary.value: The Act of Supremacy of 1534 declared Henry VIII the 'Supreme Head of the Church of England', completing the English Reformation's institutional break from Rome. The act was the culmination of Henry's campaign to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon, which Pope Clement VII had refused to grant. The monasteries were dissolved in 1536–40, their wealth transferred to the Crown. The Reformation under Henry was doctrinally conservative — he maintained Catholic theology while rejecting papal authority — but it opened the door to more thoroughgoing Protestant reform under Edward VI.

people_involved.role: Queen of England; rallied forces at Tilbury

summary.value: The Spanish Armada, a fleet of 130 ships sent by Philip II of Spain to invade England and restore Catholicism, was defeated in the summer of 1588. The English fleet under Howard and Drake harassed the Armada through the Channel; the decisive engagement came off Gravelines on 8 August, and the remnant of the Spanish fleet was scattered and largely destroyed by storms rounding the British Isles. Elizabeth I's speech to her troops at Tilbury — 'I know I have the body of a weak and feeble woman, but I have the heart and stomach of a king' — became one of the most celebrated passages of English royal rhetoric.

people_involved.role: Target of the plot; Parliament saved

summary.value: The Gunpowder Plot of 5 November 1605 was a conspiracy by a group of English Catholics, led by Robert Catesby and including Guy Fawkes, to blow up the Palace of Westminster during the State Opening of Parliament, killing King James I and the assembled Lords and Commons. The plot was discovered when an anonymous letter warned a Catholic peer to stay away; Fawkes was arrested in the cellars beneath Parliament. The failure of the plot intensified anti-Catholic sentiment in England, and its commemoration on Bonfire Night became an annual tradition.

people_involved.role: Royalist commander and defeated king

summary.value: The English Civil War (1642–49) was fought between the forces of King Charles I (Royalists, 'Cavaliers') and those of Parliament ('Roundheads'), primarily over the extent of royal power and religious settlement. Parliament's New Model Army, led by Oliver Cromwell and Thomas Fairfax, defeated the Royalists decisively at Naseby (June 1645). Charles was tried for treason — an unprecedented act — convicted, and executed on 30 January 1649. England was declared a Commonwealth under parliamentary rule, then a Protectorate under Oliver Cromwell from 1653 to 1659.

people_involved.role: Deposed king
Invited to invade; became king

summary.value: The Glorious Revolution of 1688 replaced the Catholic King James II with the Protestant William III of Orange and Mary II without significant military conflict. William landed at Brixham on 5 November 1688; James's army dissolved and he fled to France in December, which Parliament subsequently construed as abdication. The Bill of Rights (1689) codified parliamentary sovereignty, prohibited a Catholic monarch, and established the constitutional framework that still underpins British governance. The revolution was 'glorious' in Whig mythology for its bloodlessness (in England) and its constitutional settlement.

people_involved.role: Commander of the West Saxon forces; victor

summary.value: Alfred's decisive defeat of the Danish Great Heathen Army under Guthrum at Edington (Wiltshire) in May 878 ended the Danish occupation of Wessex. Guthrum was forced to accept baptism, with Alfred as his godfather, and agreed to withdraw to East Anglia under the subsequent Treaty of Wedmore. The victory was the turning point in Alfred's campaign against the Vikings and secured the survival of the West Saxon kingdom.

people_involved.role: West Saxon king and negotiator

summary.value: Following the Battle of Edington, Alfred and the Danish king Guthrum agreed terms at Wedmore. Guthrum accepted Christian baptism and withdrew to East Anglia. The treaty, later codified as the Treaty of Alfred and Guthrum, divided England along a boundary running roughly from the Thames estuary to the Dee, separating Alfred's kingdom from the Danelaw. It established a framework of coexistence between the West Saxon kingdom and Danish settlers.

people_involved.role: First King of all England

summary.value: In 927 Æthelstan expelled the Norse king Olaf Sihtricson from York and received the submission of the kings of Scotland, Strathclyde, and Wales at Eamont Bridge. This is conventionally taken as the moment England was first united under a single ruler. Æthelstan thereafter styled himself 'king of the whole of Britain' in charters, a claim reflecting his acknowledged overlordship over the other rulers of the island.

people_involved.role: Commander of the English forces; victor

summary.value: Æthelstan and his brother Edmund defeated a coalition of Norse, Scots, and Strathclyde Welsh forces at Brunanburh in 937. The exact location of the battle remains debated by historians. The victory consolidated English unity and removed the most significant challenge to English hegemony over Britain. It was celebrated in an Old English poem preserved in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, one of the finest pieces of Old English poetry to survive.

people_involved.role: King of England at the time of the battle

summary.value: The Battle of Maldon, fought in August 991, resulted in the defeat and death of the English forces under Ealdorman Byrhtnoth by Viking raiders in Essex. The battle is commemorated in the Old English poem The Battle of Maldon, one of the finest surviving pieces of Old English heroic poetry. The defeat led directly to the first payment of Danegeld, establishing a precedent of tribute payment that proved costly to the kingdom.

people_involved.role: King who authorised the payment

summary.value: Following the Battle of Maldon, Æthelred II agreed to pay the first Danegeld — a tribute of 10,000 pounds of silver to the Danish raiders — on the advice of Archbishop Sigeric of Canterbury. The practice of purchasing peace from Viking raiders, while effective in the short term, proved unsustainable and was repeatedly criticised by contemporaries including the chronicler Æthelweard.

people_involved.role: King who ordered the killings

summary.value: On 13 November 1002 (the feast of St Brice), King Æthelred II ordered the killing of Danes living in England. The massacre, which is believed to have included Gunhilde, said to be the sister of Sweyn Forkbeard of Denmark, provoked a series of devastating Danish invasions that eventually led to Sweyn's conquest of England in 1013 and Cnut's final conquest in 1016.

people_involved.role: King of England from November 1016

summary.value: Following the death of Edmund Ironside on 30 November 1016, the witan recognised Cnut as King of all England, ending the contested dual kingship established at Olney. Cnut's reign (1016–1035) was notably stable: he issued law codes building on Anglo-Saxon precedents, patronised English monasteries, made a pilgrimage to Rome in 1027, and sought to integrate himself into the existing administrative structure of the kingdom.

people_involved.role: King of England; commander of the English forces; victor

summary.value: On 25 September 1066, Harold II defeated and killed the Norwegian king Harald Hardrada and his own brother Tostig at Stamford Bridge, Yorkshire. The victory eliminated the Scandinavian claim to England but exhausted Harold's army. Receiving news three days later of William of Normandy's landing at Pevensey on 28 September, Harold marched south immediately, denying himself time to rest and reinforce his forces before the engagement at Hastings.

value.notes: Acceded to the throne of Wessex after the death of his brother Æthelred I, probably in April 871.

people_involved.role: New King of Wessex

summary.value: Alfred succeeded his brother Æthelred I as King of Wessex in 871, a year of almost continuous warfare against Danish armies. He inherited a kingdom under severe military pressure and a treasury depleted by tribute payments. Over the following decades Alfred transformed Wessex into a state capable of resisting and eventually turning back the Great Heathen Army, laying the administrative and cultural foundations of the English kingdom.

value.notes: Died 26 October 899; buried at Winchester.

people_involved.role: King of Wessex; died

summary.value: Alfred died on 26 October 899 and was buried at Winchester. He had reigned for twenty-eight years, during which he repelled the Danish occupation of Wessex, reorganised the defence of his kingdom through the burh system, and sponsored a systematic programme of translation of Latin learning into Old English. He was succeeded by his son Edward the Elder. No other English ruler has been styled 'the Great' by later tradition.

value.notes: Crowned at Bath on Whit Sunday, 11 May 973.

people_involved.role: King of England; consecrated

summary.value: Edgar, who had already ruled England for over a decade, was formally consecrated king at Bath on Whit Sunday 973 in a ceremony devised by Archbishop Dunstan. The rite, held deliberately at the age of thirty following the canonical model for priestly ordination, set out the obligations of Christian kingship and established the coronation ordo still used in modified form at English and British coronations today. Weeks later, at Chester, Edgar received the submission of six or eight sub-kings who rowed him on the River Dee.

value.notes: Killed at Corfe Castle, Dorset, 18 March 978.

people_involved.role: King of England; murdered
Half-brother; successor

summary.value: Edward the Martyr was killed at Corfe Castle on 18 March 978 while visiting his half-brother Æthelred. The identity of his murderers was never established, but members of the nobility supporting Æthelred's succession were widely suspected. Edward was buried at Wareham without royal ceremony, later translated to Shaftesbury Abbey, where he became the focus of a cult. His death brought his twelve-year-old half-brother Æthelred to the throne, beginning a troubled reign during which Danish raids intensified.

value.notes: Sweyn accepted as king by most of England by late 1013; Æthelred II fled to Normandy.

people_involved.role: Conqueror; King of England
Deposed king; fled to Normandy

summary.value: In 1013 Sweyn Forkbeard, King of Denmark, launched a full-scale invasion of England and was accepted as king across much of the country. Æthelred II fled to Normandy, the first English king to be driven from his throne since the Viking settlements of the ninth century. Sweyn's triumph was short-lived: he died on 3 February 1014, whereupon the witan recalled Æthelred. It nonetheless demonstrated the fragility of the late Anglo-Saxon kingdom and presaged the conquest completed by Sweyn's son Cnut two years later.

people_involved.role: Lancastrian king in whose name the army fought

summary.value: At Sandal Castle near Wakefield, Richard Duke of York was lured out of his fortification by a Lancastrian force under the Duke of Somerset and the Earl of Wiltshire that heavily outnumbered his own. York was killed in the fighting and his son Edmund, Earl of Rutland, was cut down fleeing across a bridge. York's severed head was set above the gates of the city, wearing a paper crown in mockery of his royal pretensions. The defeat was catastrophic for the Yorkist cause but galvanised Edward, now Earl of March, to press his own claim to the throne with redoubled energy.

people_involved.role: Yorkist commander; shortly to be proclaimed King Edward IV

summary.value: Edward, the eighteen-year-old heir to the Yorkist cause after his father's death at Wakefield, routed a Lancastrian force under Jasper Tudor at Mortimer's Cross in the Welsh Marches. Before the battle a parhelion — three suns appearing in the sky — was interpreted by Edward as a divine omen of the Holy Trinity blessing his cause. He adopted the Sun in Splendour as his personal badge thereafter. Owen Tudor, stepfather of Henry VI and grandfather of the future Henry VII, was captured and beheaded at Hereford after the battle, refusing to believe until the axe was raised that he would actually be executed.

value.notes: Palm Sunday.

people_involved.role: Yorkist commander; won the crown decisively here
Lancastrian king; fled north to Scotland after the defeat

summary.value: Fought in a blizzard on Palm Sunday 1461, Towton was arguably the bloodiest single day of battle in English history, with estimates ranging from 20,000 to 28,000 dead. Edward IV had issued orders that no quarter was to be given. A snowstorm blowing from the south blinded the Lancastrian archers and carried their arrows short; the Yorkist arrows fell true. A Lancastrian cavalry force that arrived late struck the already-routing army from the rear, turning rout into annihilation. Henry VI fled to Scotland. Edward's victory was so comprehensive that it secured his crown for a decade and eliminated most of the senior Lancastrian leadership.

value.notes: Easter Sunday.

people_involved.role: Yorkist king; returned from Burgundian exile to reclaim his crown
Lancastrian king; briefly restored; returned to the Tower after Barnet

summary.value: Edward IV, returned from exile at the Burgundian court, met the army of Richard Neville, Earl of Warwick — the man who had made and then sought to unmake him — at Barnet on Easter Sunday 1471. A heavy pre-dawn fog caused fatal confusion: Lancastrian troops on the right mistook their Oxford allies for Yorkists and attacked them. The Oxford contingent fled the field raising cries of treason. In the rout that followed, Warwick himself was killed — the most powerful magnate of his age, who had crowned and deposed two kings. Henry VI, briefly restored, was taken back to the Tower.

people_involved.role: Yorkist king; delivered the killing blow to the Lancastrian cause
Lancastrian king; died in the Tower of London on 21 May 1471, almost certainly murdered

summary.value: Three weeks after Barnet, Edward IV destroyed the last Lancastrian army at Tewkesbury on 4 May 1471. Edward of Westminster, Prince of Wales — the only son of Henry VI and the last Lancastrian heir of the main line — was killed in or immediately after the battle, aged seventeen. Lancastrian commanders who fled into Tewkesbury Abbey were dragged out and executed on Edward's orders. Henry VI was found dead in the Tower of London on the night of 21 May — officially of 'pure displeasure and melancholy,' but almost certainly murdered. The House of Lancaster was extinct in the male line.

title: Death of Henry II at Chinon

people_involved.role: Angevin king; died broken by his sons' rebellion
Eldest surviving son; led the rebellion against his father

summary.value: The greatest Plantagenet king died at Chinon Castle on 6 July 1189, defeated and humiliated by his eldest surviving son Richard, acting in alliance with Philip II of France. Henry had spent his final months trying to negotiate peace while his empire crumbled around him. When he learned that his youngest and most beloved son John — for whom he had sacrificed so much — had joined the rebellion, he turned his face to the wall and refused food. His reported last words were 'Shame, shame on a conquered king.' Richard reportedly wept when he gazed upon his father's body, laid out at Fontevraud.

title: Fall of Château Gaillard and the Loss of Normandy

value.notes: Château Gaillard fell to Philip II after six months.

people_involved.role: King of England; failed to relieve Château Gaillard or prevent the loss of Normandy

summary.value: Philip II of France captured Château Gaillard — the supposedly impregnable castle Richard I had built in a single year — after a six-month siege on 6 March 1204. King John, distracted by personal quarrels and lacking Richard's military energy, made no serious effort to relieve it. The fall opened the way for Philip to conquer all of Normandy by the summer. The loss ended 138 years of Anglo-Norman lordship on the continent and was the defining catastrophe of John's reign. To recover the lost territories, John would raise oppressive taxes, provoking the baronial revolt that produced Magna Carta eleven years later.

value.notes: Harfleur surrendered on this date; Agincourt followed on 25 October.

people_involved.role: English king; led the siege in person

summary.value: Henry V's 1415 French campaign opened with a five-week siege of the fortified port of Harfleur at the mouth of the Seine. The town surrendered on 22 September, but dysentery — the medieval army's most lethal enemy — had decimated Henry's force during the siege, costing him perhaps a quarter of his men before he even marched to Agincourt. Henry expelled much of the French population and garrisoned the town as an English bridgehead. Shakespeare gave the assault its most famous expression in Henry V: 'Once more unto the breach, dear friends, once more, / Or close the wall up with our English dead.'

Scotland's greatest military disaster. James IV of Scotland, honouring the Franco-Scottish Alliance while Henry VIII campaigned in France, invaded northern England with the largest Scottish army ever assembled. The English force under Thomas Howard, Earl of Surrey, met the Scots at Flodden Field in Northumberland on 9 September 1513. James's pike formations were devastatingly effective on flat ground but unsuited to the broken terrain of Flodden Edge; English bills — short-handled slashing weapons — cut through the Scottish pikes. James IV himself was killed, becoming the last British monarch to die in battle. Approximately 10,000 Scots died, including the king, three bishops, eleven earls, and fifteen lords.

people_involved.role: Queen of England; her Spanish marriage had drawn England into the war

summary.value: On 7 January 1558, the Duke of Guise's French army captured Calais after a siege of less than a week. Calais had been in English hands for 211 years — seized by Edward III after the Battle of Crécy in 1347 — and served as England's main wool staple port and its chief continental bridgehead. Queen Mary I's Spanish marriage had dragged England into the Habsburg-Valois war, and the garrison had been left undermanned and under-resourced. Mary was devastated by the loss and reportedly declared that when she died, 'Calais' would be found engraved upon her heart. It was England's last territorial possession in France, and its loss was not reversed.

people_involved.role: Royalist king; lost effective control of the North after this defeat

summary.value: The largest battle of the English Civil War was fought on Marston Moor west of York on 2 July 1644. A combined Parliamentarian and Scottish Covenanting army routed the Royalist force of Prince Rupert and the Marquess of Newcastle. Oliver Cromwell's cavalry — already called the 'Ironsides' — proved decisive: unlike royalist horse, they maintained order after their charge and wheeled to strike the Royalist infantry from the flank. York surrendered days later. Cromwell declared it 'an absolute defeat of the enemy.' The North of England was effectively lost to the King, marking a turning point from which the Royalist cause never fully recovered.

people_involved.role: Royalist king; his correspondence was captured after the battle

summary.value: The decisive engagement of the First Civil War, fought near Naseby in Northamptonshire on 14 June 1645. The New Model Army under Sir Thomas Fairfax and Oliver Cromwell annihilated the last main Royalist field army. Prince Rupert's cavalry swept the Parliamentarian left but galloped on to plunder the baggage, leaving the Royalist infantry exposed to flanking attack. Among the captured baggage was the king's own correspondence — letters revealing that Charles had been secretly negotiating with Irish Catholics and soliciting foreign troops, fatally damaging his reputation as a man who could be trusted. Within a year the first war was over.

value.notes: Trial opened 20 January; verdict delivered 27 January.

people_involved.role: Defendant; refused throughout to recognise the court's jurisdiction

summary.value: On 20 January 1649, the Rump Parliament — purged of moderates by Colonel Pride in December 1648 — convened a High Court of Justice at Westminster Hall to try the King of England for high treason against his own people. Charles I refused to enter a plea or recognise the court's authority: 'I would know by what power I am called hither, I would know by what authority, I mean lawful authority.' He argued that no human court could possess jurisdiction over an anointed king, and that to concede the point would destroy the liberty of every subject. He was found guilty and condemned to death on 27 January by 59 of the 67 commissioners present.

people_involved.role: King of England; executed by beheading

summary.value: On 30 January 1649, King Charles I was led from St James's Palace through the park to the Banqueting House on Whitehall — its ceiling painted by Rubens with an apotheosis of his father James I. He stepped through a window onto a scaffold to be beheaded before a crowd of thousands. Charles wore two shirts so that he would not shiver from cold and be mistaken for trembling in fear. He made a brief speech asserting that he was an innocent man, a martyr to the people's liberty, and a Christian. His last reported word was 'Remember.' The crowd gave out a collective groan when the axe fell. It was the first and only public execution of a reigning English monarch.

people_involved.role: Charles II, crowned King of Scots at Scone; fled into exile after his defeat

summary.value: Cromwell called Worcester his 'crowning mercy.' On 3 September 1651 his veteran army annihilated the force of Charles II — recently crowned King of Scots at Scone — at Worcester, ending the Civil Wars definitively. Few English Royalists had joined Charles's Scottish army, and the city's walls provided no protection against Cromwell's superior numbers. Charles spent six weeks as a hunted fugitive: hiding in a hollow oak at Boscobel House, disguised as a servant, sheltered at great personal risk by Catholic gentry. He eventually escaped from Shoreham to France, where he would remain in exile for nine years until the Restoration.

value.notes: Charles II's thirtieth birthday.

people_involved.role: King of England; restored to his throne after eleven years of exile

summary.value: On 29 May 1660 — his thirtieth birthday — Charles II rode into London to tumultuous popular celebration, restoring the Stuart monarchy after eleven years of republic and military rule. The Protectorate had disintegrated after Oliver Cromwell's death in 1658 and his son Richard's resignation. General George Monck, commanding the army in Scotland, had marched south, reconstituted the Long Parliament, and organised the Convention Parliament. Charles entered through Whitehall and received the proclamations of the assembled council. Samuel Pepys recorded in his diary: 'The shouting and the joy expressed by all is past imagination.' The regicides would be hunted down; thirteen were executed.
